my_sentence: The HR department will according to the result.
correction: The HR department will decide based on the results. / The result will depend on the exam.
explanation: After the modal 'will' you need a base verb, but 'according to' is a preposition, not a verb. Either add a verb ('will decide/act according to ...') or restructure with a real verb such as 'depend on'.
correct_examples: The decision will depend on your score.
HR will act according to the results.
context: Explaining how a company technology exam result is used

my_sentence: It's just waste my time.
correction: It just wastes my time.
explanation: The subject 'it' takes the third-person singular verb 'wastes'. Don't insert 'is' before a plain present-tense action verb ('It's waste' = 'It is waste'), which is ungrammatical.
correct_examples: It just wastes my time.
This kind of exam only wastes our time.
context: Saying the exam was not useful for him

my_sentence: If someone tries to do that, he or she will be sent to prison for many years.
correction: If someone tries to do that, he or she can be sent to prison for many years.
explanation: Use 'can be' for a possible consequence/general rule rather than 'will be', which states it as a certainty. The penalty is a possibility, so 'can be sent' is more accurate.
correct_examples: Anyone who does that can be sent to prison.
Offenders can be jailed for several years.
context: Talking about gun laws and punishment in China

my_sentence: If you do it, you will get serious trouble.
correction: If you do it, you'll get into serious trouble.
explanation: The fixed collocation is 'get into trouble' — you need the preposition 'into'. 'Get serious trouble' is missing it.
correct_examples: You'll get into trouble if you do that.
He got into serious trouble with the police.
context: Consequences of owning a gun illegally

my_sentence: If you do not have permission to own a gun, you can be published up to three to seven years.
correction: If you do not have permission to own a gun, you can be jailed for three to seven years.
explanation: The word is 'punished'/'jailed', not 'published' (a confusable sound). Also, a length of time after a sentence takes 'for': 'jailed for X years', not 'up to ... years' without a preposition.
correct_examples: He can be jailed for up to seven years.
Offenders are punished with several years in prison.
context: Penalty for illegally owning a gun

my_sentence: The government wants to keep stability.
correction: The government wants to keep the stability of society. / The government wants to maintain social stability.
explanation: When 'stability' refers to a specific thing (society's), it needs the article + an 'of' phrase: 'the stability of society'. Alternatively use the adjective form 'social stability'.
correct_examples: They want to maintain the stability of society.
The policy is meant to preserve social stability.
context: Why the government restricts gun ownership

my_sentence: Police officers and soldiers can own the gun.
correction: Police officers and soldiers can own guns.
explanation: For a general statement about a category, use the bare plural 'guns', not 'the gun'. 'The gun' points to one specific gun.
correct_examples: Only soldiers can own guns.
Police officers are allowed to carry firearms.
context: Who is allowed to own a gun

my_sentence: If a lot of people arranged together, the government just needs to send five or ten officers.
correction: Even if hundreds of people gather together, they can be controlled by five to ten officers.
explanation: People come together = 'gather', not 'arrange'. 'Arrange' means to organize or put things in order; it does not mean a crowd assembling.
correct_examples: A crowd gathered in the square.
Even if thousands gather, the police can manage them.
context: Why an unarmed crowd is easy to control

my_sentence: If you have a gun, no one want to hurt you.
correction: If you have a gun, no one will want to hurt you. / no one wants to hurt you.
explanation: 'No one' is third-person singular, so it takes 'wants', or add the modal 'will' for a prediction: 'no one will want to'.
correct_examples: No one wants to be the last to leave.
If you are armed, no one will dare to attack you.
context: Arguing that owning a gun deters attackers

my_sentence: They are in the same level, no one is stronger than others.
correction: They are on the same level; no one is stronger than the others.
explanation: (assistant note) The collocation is 'on the same level', not 'in'. Also use 'the others' when referring to the rest of a known group.
correct_examples: With guns, everyone is on the same level.
The two teams are on the same level.
context: Claiming guns make everyone equal

my_sentence: For ordinary people, they don't allow to own guns.
correction: Ordinary people are not allowed to own guns.
explanation: Ordinary people are the ones being restricted, so use the passive 'are not allowed to'. The active 'they don't allow to' has no object and reverses who does the allowing.
correct_examples: Civilians are not allowed to carry weapons.
Students are not allowed to leave early.
context: Whether ordinary people in Japan can own guns

my_sentence: In the countryside, they don't have enough people.
correction: In the countryside, there aren't enough people.
explanation: To say something exists (or doesn't) in a place, use the existential 'there is/are', not 'they have'. 'They don't have' needs a specific possessor.
correct_examples: There aren't enough people in the village.
There were too many cars on the road.
context: Explaining firecrackers are fine in the countryside

my_sentence: My daughter wants to see some fireworks, so I bought some.
correction: My daughter wanted to see some fireworks, so I bought some.
explanation: Since you are narrating a past event (you already bought them), the first verb should also be past: 'wanted', to agree with 'bought'.
correct_examples: She wanted to watch the show, so I booked tickets.
He was hungry, so I made him a sandwich.
context: Why he bought fireworks years ago

my_sentence: My daughter wanted to see some fireworks, so I bought for her.
correction: My daughter wanted to see some fireworks, so I bought them for her.
explanation: 'Buy' is transitive here and needs a direct object: 'bought them'. Don't drop the object pronoun even when it's clear from context.
correct_examples: I bought them for her.
She liked the flowers, so he bought some for her.
context: Buying fireworks for his daughter

my_sentence: If you want to use them, you need to have a special processor.
correction: If you want to use them, you need to undergo a special process.
explanation: 'Process' is the procedure/steps; 'processor' is a device (e.g. a CPU). You mean the procedure, so use 'process'.
correct_examples: You must go through a special process to get a license.
The approval process takes two weeks.
context: Owning a collectible blade legally

my_sentence: They will become a sharpness.
correction: They will become sharp.
explanation: After 'become' use the adjective 'sharp' to describe a quality. 'Sharpness' is the noun form and takes no article here; 'a sharpness' is wrong.
correct_examples: The blade became sharp after grinding.
The picture became clear.
context: How a blade is processed to be sharp

my_sentence: Everyone need buy some knife to cut fruit and vegetables.
correction: Everyone needs to buy a knife to cut fruit and vegetables.
explanation: (assistant note) 'Everyone' is singular, so 'needs'; and 'need to' takes the to-infinitive: 'needs to buy'. Use the singular 'a knife' for one ordinary knife.
correct_examples: Everyone needs to buy a ticket.
She needs to take a knife to cut it.
context: Arguing that owning kitchen knives is normal

my_sentence: The government reviews the films before they show on TV or online.
correction: The government reviews the films before they are shown on TV or online.
explanation: (assistant note) The films are shown by someone, so use the passive 'are shown'. 'Before they show' would mean the films do the showing themselves.
correct_examples: The movie is reviewed before it is shown.
The data is checked before it is published.
context: How films are screened/censored before release

my_sentence: There are many films that was banned in my country.
correction: There are many films that were banned in my country.
explanation: (assistant note) The relative clause refers to 'films' (plural), so the verb must agree: 'that were banned'.
correct_examples: There are many books that were banned.
These are the films that were censored.
context: Listing banned violent films

my_sentence: There are too many violence in it, bloody and violence, so it was banned.
correction: There is too much violence and blood in it, so it was banned.
explanation: (assistant note) 'Violence' is uncountable, so use 'too much', not 'too many', and a singular verb 'there is'. Use the noun 'blood' (not 'bloody', an adjective) in the list of nouns.
correct_examples: There is too much violence in the film.
The movie has too much blood and gore.
context: Why a Jackie Chan film was banned in China
